chapters.title: Rule zero: there are no rules

rules.label: Wizard's first rule, corollary
rules.text: Your creation will probably outlive you, so document it.

rules.label: Wizard's second rule
rules.text: When intuition becomes unreliable, build a table. Then let the table reason for you.

rules.label: Wizard's third rule
rules.text: Before you try to convince people, ask which language their problem already speaks.

rules.label: Wizard's fourth rule
rules.text: When a problem feels too large, do not only look for a larger solution. Look for the smaller shape that changes what kind of problem it is.

rules.label: Wizard's fifth rule
rules.text: Just because it looks simple, doesn't mean it is. Every format is a bet about the shape of future problems.

rules.label: Wizard's sixth rule
rules.text: A prototype is not always a throwaway version of the real thing. Sometimes it is the first piece of ground the future manages to stand on.

rules.label: Wizard's seventh rule
rules.text: Code, like language, should clarify, not hide intent.

rules.label: Wizard's eighth rule
rules.text: Whenever you name something, you are teaching the next reader a word. Make sure the word earns its place.

rules.label: Wizard's ninth rule
rules.text: When you invent a language, first look for the smallest change in shape that lets the old problem become smaller. A new border is powerful only when it opens more than it closes.

rules.label: Wizard's tenth rule
rules.text: A reusable component is not a piece with many uses. It is a piece with few assumptions and a clear boundary.

rules.label: Wizard's eleventh rule
rules.text: Do not only ask what a thing depends on. Ask where that dependency should be bound.

rules.label: Wizard's twelfth rule
rules.text: Perfect is the enemy of good enough because the world does not wait for perfect.

rules.label: Wizard's thirteenth rule
rules.text: A closed system must solve every problem itself. An open system needs boundaries clear enough for the world to bring the next answer.

rules.label: Wizard's fourteenth rule
rules.text: Data becomes more useful when its meanings can travel farther than the program that first wrote it.

rules.label: Wizard's fifteenth rule
rules.text: Put the boundary where replacement should be cheap. If the user owns their identity and data, software can stay small enough to replace.

anchors.label: This is not a programming book
There are a lot of books about programming. Many of them are called a variant of Programming for Dummies. This is not on
This book is for people who suspect software could be simpler, stranger, smaller, and more humane than it usually is.
That is what I mean by wizard here. Not a rank, not a credential, and not a secret club. A wizard is someone willing to
You may already have been programming computers for a while. You may not be happy about the state of software and progra
To really learn what you can do, chapters in this book will go back and try to highlight where we are and how we got the
That is the point of this book.
Not that you should reject everything. Wizards don't waste good magic. They steal, improve, combine, and sometimes very
The wizard's question
A beginner asks: How do I solve this?
A better programmer asks: What is the simplest solution?
A wizard asks something stranger:
This is not laziness. Or at least not only laziness. It is the ability to look at the shape of a problem and suspect tha
Most of the chapters in this book are stories about people doing exactly that. They changed the notation. They changed t
And after that, everyone else said the new thing was obvious.
That is how magic usually works.
Same Problem Different World
Numbers: hiding calculations in symbols
How do you make a number bigger than your fingers?
That sounds like a childish question, but it is one of the first great programming questions. You have a thing in the wo
Numbers are a good place to begin because they look natural. They are not. Number systems are inventions. Interfaces. Li
Numbers, numbers, numbers
Its no secret that programming is based on numbers. Two of them, 0 and 1. It may come as a surprise to some that one of
Lets go back, way back, back into time, when the only people who existed were cavemen, cavewomen, troglodytes.
These, our ancestors, probably counted using their fingers. Holding up one or more fingers, you can count to 10 using tw
So the first number we used, was 1, or I. But tally marks get cumbersome quickly. So someone invented a new symbol to ma
And so it stayed for quite a while. Untill a roman (or probably someone else and the romans just stole the idea) had the
What the romans invented here was that you could write a calculation to describe the number. 4 is written as V minus I.
The simple rule is that smaller marks before larger marks mean subtraction. If they are placed after larger marks, they
So as an example, what does the Roman numeral MMXXI translate to in our Hindu-Arabic numerals? (Yes, they are arabic in
Notice we've come from the cavemen period, at least 35.000 years ago all the way to the romans, and we still have to do
But lets step back a bit and introduce the abacus. This simple devices' origins are unknown. It could be that its been i
This may be a good first lesson of programming for wizards: it is better to steal a good idea than to come up with a wor
Anyway, back to the abacus. Lets show one for those who haven't seen one yet:
This is a chinese version, you could probably find its like still in use all over the world today. The idea is simple, e
But much more important is that here, again, the system introduces a calculation into the representation of numbers. Its
It is lot easier to explain if I use arabic numerals. They use the exact same multiplication by column as the abacus doe
And here we have for the first time the number 0. Without it, arabic numerals are impossible.
As a side note, the idea of a number 0 is much older than this. But its status was not well established. How can a numbe
As another aside, I should probably call the arabic numerals hindu numerals instead. It seems the arabs stole the idea f
Now back to large numbers. Using the position to multipy each numeral to describe large numbers, we can create very larg
But there is another calculation hidden inside these numbers. This is called 'modular arithmetic'. The idea is that we c
Why is this important? Well, this insight opens the way for the first calculator. A mechanical device that can add and s
Blaise Pascal is credited with making one of the first usable mechanical calculators. It could add and subtract, and wit
I should mention the Antikythera device. This is a device from the classic greek era, which is purported to be able to c
To really understand how much of a leap this is, just take a look back at our timeline of numbers. Around 100BC is befor
This leads to another lesson hiding inside the first one: your creation will probably outlive you, so document it.
We're getting pretty good at writing large numbers. We can write down 1,000,000 and read it without blinking an eye. But
To write that number down you will need a number with at least 111 0's. Clearly that is no longer practical. So enter sc
math block
But we can also use these to explain the positional nature of arabic numerals:
table
So the number 972 is equal to $$ 9 \times 10^2 + 7 \times 10^1 +2 \times 10^0 $$
So what about our original two numbers, 1 and 0. Well, if you understand the magic of positional notation, it turns out
You can use any amount of numbers, like 16 for hexadecimal numbers, e.g:
math block
$0x$ is just a convention to say 'this is a hexadecimal number', and since there are 16 single position numerals, we've
Or octal, with 8 numerals per position:
math block
Or, minimize it two two numbers, 1 and 0 and you get binary:
math block
And using exponents, we can explain it a bit better:
table
Which means that the binary number 101 is equal to
math block
This simplifies to
math block
Binary numbers are so ubiquitous in programming, that you'll see the powers of 2 around a lot. So here are the first few
table
This is a good time to end our archaeological dig through numbers. There is quite a bit more to explore, but you can do
The important thing for now is this: every number system we saw did work for us. Tally marks counted. Roman numerals emb
A notation is not just a way to write something down. A good notation performs part of the work for you. And most good n
Numbers Are Machines
Wizard's first rule
Wizard's first rule, corollary
Logic: turning truth into machinery
You are standing before two doors. One door will lead to your desire, the other to your doom. Before the doors are two g
However, you may only ask one question.
This is a famous riddle, with a single correct answer. An answer you can reach by rigorously applying logic. Boolean log
Obviously you need to ask one of the guards a question that will reveal which door to pass through. But if you ask eithe
table
Without more information, you cannot know which door to use. However, you can ask a slightly more convoluted question, t
The question is:
Now whatever answer you get, you choose the other door.
If the guard you ask tells the truth, she must give you the wrong answer, since that is what the other guard would say.
No matter which guard you ask, you will get the wrong door. So you choose the other one.
Truth Table Riddle
Boolean logic
Today we call this Boolean Logic, named after the 19th century wizard George Boole. And the tables above are called trut
But Boolean logic is at the core of modern programming. It is at the core of computers. In a sense all things your compu
The operations described by Boolean logic are mostly applied on two terms. Lets call them p and q. Just like our guards,
The operations are: and, or, not, and iff.
table
table
table
And of course:
table
Combining these, we can also create nor and nand.
table
table
Wait a minute... what is this nand thing? Why not just write not and?
Well... it so happens that your computer can implement everything with just nand. All other operations can be written as
This is the second great trick in this chapter. First we turned a riddle into a table. Then we turned the table into a m
That is a very wizardly move. When intuition gets unreliable, make the possible cases explicit. Then let the table reaso
Nand All The Way Down
Wizard's second rule
Language: the tool that changes the thinker
Language is magic.
We're so used to it that we don't see it, but think about it. While you are reading this, I have the capability to injec
Lets start at the very beginning. A very good place to start.
We don't know exactly when humans started to use language. But we can be sure it was before we started to write. The cap
The one constant in our use of language seems to be that every generation mutates the language they use. There is no hum
An interesting question related to this is usually discussed as linguistic relativity: does language dictate what we can
Words Change The Problem
Whatever it is, we use language to communicate our ideas to each other. However, language is just a limited part of the
Another way to look at it is by dividing your brain functions into the fast and slow systems. A wizard called Daniel Kah
Looking at it this way, it is no wonder that Kahneman argues that our system 2, the slow system, is often a servant to o
A simple rule I've found to be true: you cannot change someone's decision with rational arguments if that decision was b
You, as a wizard, and specifically focused on programming, are quite unusual. You have taught yourself to think up a new
This doesn't mean that you are better, or other people are somehow worse. It does mean you are different. If you want to
Now, to be clear, I do not advocate manipulating people. There is a long and bad history of demagogues that somehow unde
The point is not that language gives you a trick for winning arguments. The point is that language is part of the proble
This matters when you talk to other people. It will matter later when we talk about programming languages as well. Code
But before we get there, we need to look at the Web. Because the Web is one of the great examples of what happens when a
Wizard's third rule
The Web as address: a spell for pointing anywhere
In 1989 a wizard called Tim invented the World Wide Web and changed the world forever.
That sounds dramatic, but 1989 was not exactly short on drama. Walls were falling. Regimes were cracking. The Cold War w
That is one of the problems with good magic. When it first appears, it often looks like paperwork.
The first website did not look like the future either. It did not have video, rounded corners, infinite scrolling, cooki
html block
It does not look revolutionary today. It barely even looks designed. But there is a little piece of that first page that
html block
The a tag is tiny. Almost suspiciously tiny. Just one letter. Hidden inside it is another invention, probably the more i
url block
The URL. The Uniform Resource Locator.
A spell for pointing anywhere.
Url As Compressed Map
Hypertext was already in the air
To understand why the URL mattered, we need to go back a little further than 1989.
The idea of hypertext was older than the Web. Ted Nelson coined the term in 1965, and you can trace the dream further ba
But there is a large gap between dreaming about a global web of knowledge and getting one that ordinary people can actua
Ted Nelson started the Xanadu project in the 1960s. It was ambitious, beautiful, and, as ambitious beautiful things ofte
HyperCard was wonderful inside its own little universe. A card could point to another card. A button could take you some
The awkward part was the boundary. What if the thing you wanted to point to was maintained by someone else? What if it l
Xanadu tried to design a grand hypertext universe. HyperCard made a smaller universe that people could actually play wit
That is the part I find interesting. Tim Berners-Lee did not have to invent networking from scratch. TCP/IP was there. H
The missing piece was a simple way for one document to say:
Over there on that machine. Over there using that protocol. Over there at that path.
A normal-looking address. With a lot of stolen machinery hiding inside it.
Three stolen pieces
Look again at the URL:
url block
It has three obvious parts:
text block
The protocol tells your computer how to talk.
The hostname tells it which name in the global naming system to resolve.
The path tells the other side what you are looking for.
Put those pieces together, and a plain text document can point to a published resource on another computer, maintained b
That is a ridiculous amount of power for one string.
And the string is still readable. This matters. It matters more than programmers sometimes admit. You can copy a URL int
The URL is an address, but it is also a tiny user interface to the network.
The path: Unix hiding in plain sight
Start with the path:
text block
If you have used a Unix-like system, this shape is familiar. A slash separates folders and files. A path starts at a roo
text block
This feels obvious now, but it was only one possible way to name files. DOS and Windows used backslashes and drive lette
text block
The URL chose the simpler Unix-shaped world.
Unix itself had stolen and simplified ideas from earlier systems such as Multics. One of its beautiful tricks is that us
That idea survives inside the URL. The path says:
text block
It does not mention a disk, a controller, a cabinet, a room, a CPU architecture or a filesystem implementation. All of t
A path is a useful lie. Like many useful lies in programming, it hides the machinery that would only get in the way.
Of course, useful lies still need accounting departments. Real names contain spaces, punctuation, accents, and all the o
That is not the glamorous part of the Web. It is the kind of small compromise that lets a simple text format survive the
The hostname: a global phone book that is not a book
Now look at the middle part:
url block
The double slash says that what follows is a network location. This convention was borrowed too; Berners-Lee has said he
Again, this is how wizards really work most of the time. They do not produce polished inventions from a vacuum. They not
The more important borrowed system here is DNS, the Domain Name System.
Before DNS, the internet was small enough that people could maintain host files: lists of names and IP addresses. Your c
DNS turned naming into a distributed system. One machine no longer needed a complete list of every other machine. Names
So info.cern.ch is doing more than naming a server. It hooks the URL into a shared global act of naming.
The URL did not solve naming. It gave DNS a place inside the hypertext spell.
The protocol: a slightly mad opening move
Finally, the first part:
url block
At first this seems like the boring bit. It tells the browser to use HTTP. Sensible enough.
But putting the scheme at the front was also a wonderfully strange move, because it left the door open for the link to e
Compare it with Gopher, another worldwide information system that appeared around the same time. For a brief moment, Gop
text block
A line like that can point to information on another Gopher server. The format is useful, but the world it imagines is s
A URL begins by saying which kind of space you are entering. http: is one possibility. A browser might also understand f
Some of these are network protocols. Some are browser instructions. Some are hooks into other applications. Some feel li
This is one of the reasons URL-shaped strings escaped the Web. APIs use them. Package managers use them. Apps use them.
That is usually a good sign. A design has become part of the language when people start using it in places its inventor
The small turn
So the URL is not impressive because every part of it was new. It is impressive because a few existing parts were turned
text block
That little shape was simple enough to write by hand, simple enough to paste into another document, and open enough that
This is where the URL feels most wizard-like to me. Xanadu tried to design the whole dream of hypertext. The URL aimed a
But the shape of the problem had changed. A document no longer had to contain the world it wanted to point into. It only
The first version of the Web could be poor, ugly, incomplete, and still useful. Perhaps especially because it was incomp
Why this matters for programming
Programmers often look for the important abstraction in the wrong place. We expect it to be a clever class hierarchy, a
Sometimes it is. Quite often it is just a string with a few separators in the right places.
The URL works because it crosses boundaries without swallowing the systems on either side. The document can contain a li
Each part keeps its own job. The connection between them stays small.
That is much harder to design than it looks. Large systems keep tempting us to make larger abstractions: one model to ex
This is something a wizard is very aware of. A small change in representation can make a problem manageable. Sometimes i
Wizard's fourth rule
The Web as document: choosing a tree
What shape should a document have?
The question sounds harmless. A document is just text, right? Perhaps text with some headings, links, emphasis, images,
HTML chose a tree.
That choice made the Web easy enough to write by hand, easy enough for browsers to recover from mistakes, and structured
As I wrote in the previous chapter, in 1989 a wizard called Tim invented the World-Wide Web and changed the world foreve
I've already talked about HyperText, so I'll focus on the Markup Language part here. Markup has been around longer than
Markup is simply an additional marking on a document, added to add extra meaning. The general idea is now called a marku
The editor added markup to tell the typesetter what font and font size to use, as well as other layout decisions.
When the first computers came around, they were very limited in the kind of output they could create. If there was a pri
Once the output devices gained more capabilities, their manufacturers quickly realized a need to somehow instruct these
One application was on how and where to display text on terminal screens. From this we ultimately end up with ANSI codes
The other, more relevant application, was to create better printed output. Early on, the only way to get decent printed
The wizards at Bell Labs, who were busy inventing Unix, were not impressed. They wanted their Unix and C manuals to be n
Another wizard, called Knuth, wanted to write a book about programming. Well actually, he wanted to write the book on pr
So in 1974 he took some time off to develop his own, better, typesetting system. That hiatus turned out to be 11 years a
Both troff and TeX use markup languages specifically to typeset documents before putting them on paper. As such they are
This is where things get interesting. Sometime in the 1970's some wizards from IBM created a system called GML. This eit
gml block
Unfortunately their work only made it out of IBM as a product you could buy and use, somewhere at the end of the 1980's.
So the award for one of the first released markup languages that described the meaning--the semantics--of a text goes to
Here is a sample of a text marked up with Scribe:
scribe block
GML eventually turned into SGML--Standard Generalized Markup Language, which became an industry standard in 1986. And in
Here is that first HTML page again:
html block
This is not an SGML document, it doesn't have a DTD header. Later HTML would become compliant with SGML, at least until
It does use the same syntax to differentiate between markup and content. All markup tags are enclosed in characters.
Let's take a closer look at the reasons behind this format. One of the main goals of the web was to make it easy for peo
This means that the source code should be human readable. So, to make the structure more clear, a markup had a start and
code block
This is technically feasible, maybe even easier. However, for a human this is much more difficult to read. And crucially
Another consequence of the chosen format is that some characters can not be used in the content anymore. Clearly the and
However, now we've added the & character to the list of special characters as well. This is easily solved:
And these are the basics of HTML.
Now for the tricky parts. As you've seen, HTML has elements that have a start and end tag. This means you can enclose ot
html block
Instead, you are supposed to write this:
html block
And this HTML can be represented as a tree structure:
This is how your web browser understands this HTML. Most modern browsers allow you to take a look at this structure, by
The HTML specification forces you to create a tree structure of your document. There can be no overlapping markup.
This tree structure does have benefits. You can also write the above HTML as follows:
html block
And it would render the same. The tree structure is now made visible by the level of indenting in the source. This is no
To make this work, web browsers apply what is called white-space collapsing. In general, any time you add multiple white
For example, both lines in the example below will line out perfectly:
html block
Even the extra 3 spaces at the start of the second line do not show up.
However, there is another tricky complication. Take the following HTML:

This renders like this:
But if I change the list items to render as inline elements, instead of block elements, you get this:
Let's change the markup to this instead:

There is no change in the normal rendering, as each item is a block element:
But now here it is inline:
And there is no space between the items anymore.
Finally if you really want to open a can of worms, try writing a rich text editor, with full WYSIWYG (What-You-See-Is-Wh
html block
However, what you are seeing is an editor, designed by Microsoft originally for Internet Explorer 5.5, with an API that
The only successful in-browser editors, that are capable of editing any HTML and deliver a good end result--as in: clean
This is a good lesson to learn: All choices have consequences. The less choices you make, the less consequences will hau
Html Chooses A Tree
Wizard's fifth rule
The Web as platform: the prototype that became a world
What happens when a prototype becomes the platform?
This chapter is about JavaScript, but it is also about a familiar software story. A demo is created in a hurry. It works
That is both terrifying and, sometimes, lucky.
Prototype Becomes Platform
The logical next part of the web to discuss, would be HTTP. However I don't think it would add much, or be interesting.
JavaScript
Lets go back a little bit, before may 1995. You are a young developer tasked with creating an interactive experience on
The only way would be to add a form. A visitor of your website would fill it in, then press submit. The form data is sen
In 1995 Netscape was king of the webbrowser hill. Microsoft Internet Explorer 1.0 would be released in august 1995, and
Obviously for this to come to fruition, the web would need to be more interactive, more programmable. So Netscape set ou
Why Netscape chose Java isn't hard to see. At the time there will still many different computer architectures in use. Wr
Enter Brendan Eich -- "At least client engineering management including Tom Paquin, Michael Toy, and Rick Schell, along
How short order? How about 10 days (and probably nights.)
Surely that was only a proof-of-concept, a demo? Well, yes, but this demo, this prototype ended up in production, virtua
However, we got uncharacteristically lucky. JavaScript was not a tuned-down, dummified version of Java. You see, Brendan
Scheme is a descendant of Lisp (and Algol.) It is nothing like Java. But as any Lisp-like language, you can change the l
As any prototype that is pushed into production too soon, JavaScript has its problems. But its origins in Scheme, and Se
In 2008 JavaScript got some positive publicity, with a book by wizard Douglas Crockford -- "Javascript: The Good Parts".
Toil and Trouble
Today it is hard not to associate the DOM (Document Object Model) with JavaScript. And that is not fair to JavaScript. T
But the DOM is still considered a compilation target. Not something that you program directly, unless you are a masochis
All this stuff makes web development so much harder than it needed to be. This is the real fallout from rushing JavaScri
A simple example is the JavaScript module system. For the longest time it didn't exist. In the browser you could mimic i
And even though there now is a clear standard module system, what we end up using is often an incompatible version of it
The browser-shaped computer
Netscape's dream did not quite happen in the way Netscape imagined. We did not all simply stop using operating systems a
But something close enough happened to be worth noticing.
A lot of what people now call "using the computer" is really using the browser. Mail, documents, calendars, maps, bankin
ChromeOS makes that idea unusually explicit. It is an operating system built around the assumption that the web is not j
Electron approaches the same idea from the other direction. If people still expect desktop applications, fine. Put a bro
Progressive Web Apps sit somewhere in the middle. They try to let web applications behave a bit more like installed soft
None of these are the final answer. They are clues.
The browser began as a way to read linked documents. JavaScript turned it into a programmable surface. Over time that su
The network starts to look less like something the computer connects to, and more like the place where the computer happ
That raises a question this chapter should not try to answer yet. If the web becomes the operating system, who owns the
A later chapter will pick up that question.
Wizard's sixth rule
Programming languages are for humans
A few chapters ago we looked at language as magic. Human language does not merely carry thoughts around; it changes what
Programming languages do the same thing, but with less tolerance for ambiguity and more semicolons.
This chapter returns to that idea, but now turns it toward programming proper. A programming language is not primarily a
Same Program Many Languages
Computer languages
A common misconception is that programming languages are designed to allow you to program computers. If that was the onl
code block
This machine code is written in octal, to make it slightly more readable for humans. But the first really usable step is
code block
And now with some labels and comments:
code block
This is the code to make a PDP-11 computer boot. It is commonly known as bootstrap code.
Even in this enhanced, more human form, this code is not very readable. Or expressive. Most humans don't code in assembl
The earliest programming languages designed for humans, that are still in use, are Fortran, Lisp and COBOL. And they rep
Cobol is designed to be as human readable as possible. It is explicitly designed to an English-like grammar. Its intende
cobol block
Believe it or not, cobol is still in use today. The code above is copied from a current (2022) manual for DB2, an IBM da
Bad ideas and bad code never die, they will infect other programmers and proliferate faster than any good idea ever will
Fortran, then, is a better language. It is the first imperative programming language. It influenced almost all of the pr
fortran block
Finally we arrive at Lisp. A wizard called John McCarthy dreamed up Lisp between 1956 and 1958. He succeeded in creating
common-lisp block
This is in fact a complete Lisp interpreter, written in Common Lisp. It is a translation of McCarthy's original, by a wi
The power of Lisp is that it allows you to write code using almost any paradigm. For anything any other programming lang
However, I lied a bit earlier. There is one other early programming language I didn't mention. And it is older than all
miranda block
These three lines of code solve the wellknown Towers of Hanoi problem. Just like Lisp, or perhaps even more so, function
Differences, commonalities and why we can't have nice things
Computer languages are more commonly known as programming languages. They are designed to make a computer do something.
Programming languages are designed, unlike human languages. However they aren't designed to make a computer do anything,
Just like human languages, a programming languages make it easier for some ideas to be expressed than others. The best p
Unfortunately thats also not true. You see, almost anyone will tell you that, yes, Lisp is the most powerful yet simple
The problem is communication. Not between the human wizard and their computer. The problem is communicating ideas betwee
The problem in general is that reading code is hard. It is much easier to write code. This sounds nonsensical, yet it is
One answer is to make programming languages bigger. Add more standard stuff. Add design patterns. Add frameworks. Make s
There is a reason this keeps happening. Shared shapes make code easier to read. If every project invents its own private
But there is a cost. Once the shared language becomes too large, the real problem starts to disappear underneath the sta
So the natural pressure on programming languages is strange. Small powerful languages are wonderful for the writer, but
Just like human languages.
A wizard should be aware of this tension. You do not escape it by choosing the one true language, because the one true l
That is where Domain Specific Languages become interesting.
A DSL is not automatically a separate language with a parser and a manual. Sometimes it is. SQL is its own world, and fo
The next chapter is an exhibit of that smaller kind of language. We will not build a grand new syntax. We will look for
Wizard's seventh rule
Every program contains a language
A program is not only written in a language. A program also creates a language.
Every variable name, function name, class name, method name, command name, file name and folder name adds a word to that
This is why reading code is hard. It is much easier to write code. When you write code, you already know the language yo
Tiny Dsl Boundary
Domain Specific Languages
Domain Specific Languages are just programming languages. In fact, FORTRAN is a DSL for math, COBOL is a DSL for ... ehm
When you start using DSL's, you don't use a single programming language. You use many of them, and glue them together to

The border question
This does not mean every DSL should be embedded in a host language.
Sometimes you want the hard border. A database server cannot run arbitrary JavaScript helper functions every time someon
A host-language DSL has its own dangers. If the conventions are too weak, it dissolves back into ordinary code. If the t

Wizard's ninth rule
The knitted castle
Why is it so difficult to make reusable software?
This is one of those questions that keeps returning, dressed in new clothes. Objects would do it. Components would do it
And then, a few years later, we discover that we have not built a Lego castle after all.
We have knitted another one.
Knitted Castle Vs Lego Castle
The famous wizard Alan Kay once said:
I think the situation is more dire than that. We're not stacking bricks. We're knitting castles.
A pyramid is at least made of separate stones. They may be heavy, badly placed, and difficult to move, but you can still
A knitted castle is different. Every part is made out of the same thread as every other part. The wall continues into th
This is funny when it is yarn.
It is less funny when it is your application.
The dream of Lego
The dream is obvious. We want software pieces that behave like Lego bricks.
A Lego brick does not care whether it is used in a castle, a spaceship, a bridge, or a thing a small child insists is de
That boringness is part of the magic.
A Lego brick is reusable because it makes very few assumptions about the thing you are building. It does not decide the
Most software components are not like that.
Most software components are more like a beautifully carved castle tower with a bit of wall still attached, some electri
You can reuse it. But now your project must have the right hill.
Every useful thing grows threads
When you first write a piece of software, it often starts clean. A function receives values and returns a value. A compo
Then the real world arrives.
The function needs configuration. The component needs styling. The module needs logging. The user must be allowed to can
None of these changes are absurd. That is the annoying part.
A knitted castle is usually not built by foolish decisions. It is built by reasonable decisions made locally.
One thread for the user. One thread for the database. One thread for the framework. One thread for the CSS. One thread f
After a while, the component no longer has a simple shape. It has a climate.
To reuse it, you do not only need the component. You need the weather system it expects to live in.
Packages are not the same as parts
JavaScript has one of the largest ecosystems of reusable packages ever created. NPM is both a miracle and a warning labe
It proves that reuse is possible. You can install a parser, a date library, a test runner, a renderer, a bundler, a colo
But a package is not automatically a Lego brick.
A package is a unit of distribution. It says: here is a thing you can download and import.
That is not the same as saying: here is a thing whose assumptions fit naturally into your problem.
A package can be reusable as a whole and still be impossible to take apart. You may like its parser but not its data mod
This is where much of the promise quietly leaks away. We have become very good at shipping bundles of software around th
The piece crosses the network easily. The idea does not always cross the boundary.
Reuse has a direction
One reason the Lego dream fails is that we talk about reuse as if all reuse were the same.
It is not.
A pure function is often easy to reuse because it asks for little. Give it input. Receive output. It does not need to kn
A file format can be reusable because many programs can agree on it without sharing the same code.
A protocol can be reusable because different systems can meet at a boundary and then go their separate ways again.
A UI component is harder. It often wants data, styling, events, state, focus rules, accessibility behavior, theme decisi
An application feature is harder still. A shopping cart is not just a list of products. It touches pricing, stock, ident
At some point you are no longer reusing a part. You are importing a small civilization.
This does not mean reuse is hopeless. It means the useful question is not:
The useful question is:
Can the calculation be reused without the UI? Can the data format be reused without the database? Can the protocol be re
A wizard does not ask for universal reuse first. Universal reuse is where many good designs go to become enormous.
A wizard looks for the boundary.
The boundary is the component
This is the part that is easy to miss.
The reusable thing is not only the code. It is the boundary around the code.
The boundary says what must be known from the outside and what may remain hidden inside. It says which data crosses it.
A bad boundary leaks the whole world.
A good boundary is boring in the right places.
This is why small libraries often age better than clever frameworks. A small library can sometimes say: give me this val
A framework often says: live here.
Living somewhere can be pleasant. A framework can give you roads, plumbing, electricity, building codes, a market square
This is also why standards matter. A standard creates a boundary that does not belong to one package. HTML, URLs, JSON,
The Web itself works because many different pieces agree just enough to meet each other.
Fighting for the Lego castle
So what can you do?
You cannot defeat complexity once and for all. That is the sort of promise that sells books, frameworks and conference t
But you can fight.
You can keep asking what assumptions a piece is carrying. You can make dependencies explicit instead of letting them see
You can make small languages for the stable parts of the problem, as we did with JAQT. Not always new languages with par
You can resist the urge to make every component helpful in advance. Helpfulness is where assumptions breed. A component
You can also accept that some things should not be reusable. A piece of software that is deeply fitted to one situation
The Lego castle is not built by magic universal parts. It is built by choosing where the studs go, and then having the d
The knitted castle is always waiting. Every feature wants to tie one more loop. The wizard's work is not to avoid all lo
Wizard's tenth rule
Objects: binding data, behavior, and time
In the previous chapter we looked at the knitted castle: the strange tendency of software to grow threads. Every useful
Object Oriented Programming is one of the great historical answers to that problem. Perhaps the most successful one.
It promised a way to make software out of reusable pieces. Not just functions, not just files, but little things that ca
That promise was powerful enough that OOP took over much of the programming world. For many programmers, programming bec
And yet the knitted castle did not go away.
So I will not start with a definition of OOP. Definitions are where this subject goes to become religious. I will start
Where should behavior live?
If you have data in one place and all the code that works on that data somewhere else, the distance between the two beco
One answer is: bind the behavior to the data. Put the methods where the state is. Make the thing responsible for its own
That is one useful idea hiding inside OOP. It is not the whole answer to reusable software, but it is a real answer to a
Where Should Behavior Live
Classes and objects
A class is often explained as a blueprint. An object is the thing built from that blueprint. This is good enough for a f
The more important idea is that an object carries a small world around with it. It has state, and it has behavior that i

That lets you bind later.
Binding time
A surprising amount of software design is about binding time.
When do we decide which database to use? When do we decide which template renders this page? When do we decide which imp
If you decide too early, your code becomes rigid. If you decide too late, your code becomes vague. Wizardry is not alway
This is where factories, dependency injection containers, service locators, configuration files and plugin systems all c
Some of them help. Some of them merely move the mess to a more impressive room.
Binding Time Slider
Shell and core
One useful boundary is the shell/core split.
The core contains the rules of the program. It should know as little as possible about files, databases, networks, frame
This is not because the outside world is dirty and the core is pure. That sounds moralistic, and software is already ful
The reason is more practical. The outside world changes in different ways than the rules of your program. Databases chan
This is the same move we have seen before. Change the boundary, and the problem changes shape.
Wizard's eleventh rule
Architecture: arches, pyramids, and change
Alan Kay once compared modern software to pyramid building:
At first it sounds like the kind of thing software people say when they want to sound disappointed in the entire industr
But the metaphor is worth taking seriously.
A pyramid is not a stupid structure. It is an extremely successful one. If your goal is to build something large, stable
It is also a very expensive way to create height.
And a terrible way to create empty space.
An arch solves a different problem. It does not win by adding more material. It wins by arranging the material different
That is the part Kay's metaphor points at.
Software is very good at piling up stones.
More code. More packages. More layers. More services. More configuration. More process. More diagrams explaining why the
Sometimes the pile works. Pyramids do work. But if every new problem asks for more stone, more carrying, more coordinati
So the question for this chapter is:
What would a software arch look like?
Pyramid Arch Bazaar
Pyramids and arches
I used to think the word architecture had its roots in the word arch, which had its roots in arc. Unfortunately the worl
Still, I maintain that real architecture starts when people stop only piling material and begin using the forces inside
The Egyptians built some impressive pyramids. We still do not know every detail of how they pulled it off. There are the
The Roman arch is something else. The arch existed before the Romans, but the Romans were very good at spotting useful i
This matters because the arch is not just a shape. It is a different answer to the same world.
Gravity did not go away. Stone did not become lighter. The problem was reframed. The builder learned to arrange the piec
That is what makes the image useful for software.
A software arch would not be a prettier diagram. It would not be another layer on the pile. It would be a way of arrangi
The hard part is knowing what those forces are.
In buildings, some of the forces are literal: weight, compression, tension, wind, earthquakes, water, time. In software,
A software architecture that ignores those forces is just decoration.
Patterns from buildings
Most architecture is not made of world-famous monuments. Most buildings are ordinary buildings. Houses, shops, schools,
In 1977, Christopher Alexander and his co-authors published A Pattern Language. In 1979 Alexander followed it with The T
In one of history's little jokes, software people listened.
Human architects mostly argued about Alexander or ignored him. Software architects grabbed the idea and ran off with it.
I will not spend much time on software design patterns here. More than enough has been said about them, and some of it w
The important thing for this chapter is that Alexander gives us a second way to think about architecture. Architecture i
A pattern is not a law. It is not a recipe you apply blindly. It is a remembered shape: when the world pushes like this,
That is close to what software wants. Not universal answers, but better ways to recognize the pressure.
Software arches
Alan Kay did not merely complain about pyramids. He also spent a good part of his career trying to build something close
One later attempt was the Viewpoints Research Institute, or VPRI. One of its projects asked an almost ridiculous questio
Not just an app. A stack: graphics, networking, operating-system-like pieces, user interface, languages. The sort of thi
The VPRI answer was the STEPS project. The numbers in the report are startling. A TCP/IP implementation in hundreds of l
The trick was not simply writing cleverer C.
VPRI treated language-making as an architectural tool. Each domain got a small language fitted to that domain. The proje
OMeta was one of the tools that made this style practical. It is a language for writing languages, based on ideas from P
But OMeta itself is not the software arch.
Nor are DSLs automatically the software arch. A bad little language is just a bad little language with extra ceremony. W
The VPRI lesson is more general:
Sometimes the architecture is not the arrangement of modules. Sometimes the architecture is the arrangement of meanings.
Instead of piling up code in the same old shapes, you ask what language would make the problem smaller. That is one poss
Rustic architecture
There is another direction from Alexander that is just as useful and less glamorous.
Forget the monumental building for a moment. Think about local building. The village. The workshop. The kitchen table th
Software architecture often wants to sound like skyscrapers. The important documents use words like enterprise, platform
This is where the neat engineering story starts to blur.
If software were only a matter of arranging known forces, architecture might be simpler. But software changes the forces
A bridge does not usually make people invent a new kind of river.
Software does that sort of thing all the time.
So perhaps software architecture cannot be the art of designing the perfect structure. The perfect structure would have
This brings us to two old essays that seem, at first, to be about a different question.
Richard P. Gabriel wrote The Rise of “Worse is Better”. The uncomfortable claim is that software that is in some ways wo
Eric S. Raymond later wrote The Cathedral and the Bazaar, contrasting carefully planned cathedral-style development with
These essays are not the same, but they rhyme.
They both make software perfection look suspicious.
A working good-enough solution now is always better than a perfect solution sometime in the future.
I know. Always is a dangerous word. Good. Dangerous words wake people up.
The reason is not that bad software is noble. It is not that quality does not matter. It is that the future solution is
And by then the world may have learned to ask another question.
Scale and change
The Linux kernel makes this wonderfully confusing.
From far away, Linux is the poster child for the bazaar. Open source. Many contributors. A system shaped by the work of
From close up, it does not look like anarchy. It is an enormous body of carefully reviewed code, with maintainers, subsy
So is it a cathedral or a bazaar?
Yes.
That is the wrong question.
The useful distinction is not whether software is planned or unplanned. Large successful software is almost always both.
That is the force software architecture has to handle.
Change.
Software changes the world, and is changed by it. Any software you create has to live in an environment full of new uses
This is one of the fundamental differences between software and traditional engineering. It is also why software project
A building project can be late, expensive and politically cursed, but the ground usually does not rewrite the laws of st
That does not mean architecture is hopeless. It means architecture is less about certainty than we would like.
A good architecture does not prove that you were right. It gives you somewhere to go when you discover that you were wro
Change Arrives Before Perfect
This also leads to the next question.
If the world keeps changing, where do the useful answers come from?
Not always from inside your project. Not even usually from inside your project, if the project is interesting enough. Ot
The next chapter follows that thread.
Wizard's twelfth rule
The Web as commons: innovation happens elsewhere
The previous chapter ended with change.
Software changes the world, and then the changed world comes back for the software. That is annoying, but it is also the
So the next question is where the answers are supposed to come from.
The tempting answer is: from inside the system. Hire the right people. Build the complete platform. Design the perfect e
This is a very natural dream for programmers. We like complete worlds. We like systems where the parts fit together beca
But the world is larger than your diagram.
There is an old open source book by Ron Goldman and Richard P. Gabriel called Innovation Happens Elsewhere. The title is
No matter how clever your team is, most of the clever people are somewhere else. No matter how carefully you study your
A closed system can still be wonderful. Sometimes the closed system is exactly why the experience feels polished. Everyt
But the price is that the future has to arrive through the front gate.
If the owner of the system does not build the thing, the thing does not exist there. If an outside wizard has a better i
That is a dangerous place to put the future.
Innovation Happens Elsewhere
The castle and the bazaar again
The last chapter mentioned the cathedral and the bazaar. The image is useful, but it can also mislead.
The bazaar is not magic because it is messy. Mess by itself is just mess. Anyone who has tried to use an undocumented pi
The bazaar works when there is enough shared ground for strangers to contribute without first becoming subjects of the s
That shared ground can be source code. It can be a protocol. It can be a file format. It can be a package system. It can
The important part is that the boundary is visible. Outsiders can see where to attach their work. They can replace one p
This is why openness is not the opposite of architecture. It is a different demand on architecture.
A closed architecture asks: how do we make all the parts work together under our control?
An open architecture asks: what must stay stable so that people outside our control can still make useful things?
The second question is harder in a less glamorous way. It means saying no to private shortcuts. It means documenting thi
That is the cost of letting the outside world in.
The Web was built this way
The Web won partly because it did not require one owner to finish the whole idea.
A URL let one document point at another thing somewhere else. HTML gave documents a rough shared shape. HTTP gave browse
People built browsers. People built servers. People wrote pages. People invented terrible layout tricks. People created
Some of this was beautiful. Some of it was regrettable. That is what happens when the world gets involved.
The important point is that the Web did not have to predict all of it first.
This is also why the Web keeps absorbing other ideas. It is not because the Web is the best possible form for every kind
That last phrase, "at least in principle", is doing some work.
The Web as actually lived has many castles. Search engines, social networks, app stores, cloud platforms, ad networks an
Still, the open ground matters. Without it, the castles would be the whole world.
Small software needs a large outside
This brings us back to the knitted castle.
If a program has to do everything itself, it will grow. If it has to own the data, the identity, the editor, the storage
Small replaceable software needs the opposite assumption. It needs to assume that important things live outside it.
The address is outside it. The data may be outside it. The identity may be outside it. The next tool may be written by s
This does not remove design work. It changes the design work.
Instead of designing the entire kingdom, you design the border crossings. You decide what needs a shared name, what need
This is one of the reasons open source matters, but it is not only about source code. Source code is one way to let the
A wizard should not be ashamed of using other people's magic. That is how the craft advances. The shame is building a sy
The next boundary
There is one place where the Web still very often gets this wrong.
Applications can point to each other. Servers can talk to each other. Code can depend on code written by strangers. But
That is strange, once you notice it.
If innovation happens elsewhere, then the user should be able to benefit from tools that were invented elsewhere. But th
The next chapter takes one more step before we talk about the user's home. If outside tools are supposed to work togethe
If the network becomes the place where software happens, then the facts inside our software cannot remain trapped behind
Wizard's thirteenth rule
The Web as data: things should have addresses too

This is the URL chapter coming back in another costume.
A URL let one document point outside itself. Linked data lets one fact point outside itself.
Meaning needs names too
There is a catch, of course. There is always a catch.
It is not enough to give Hilda a Web identifier. The properties need meaning as well.

The wizardly part is not the format by itself.
The wizardly part is the boundary it makes possible.
Data that can travel
Think again about small replaceable software.
A small calendar app can be small only if it does not have to own the whole identity system, contact list and notificati
For that to work, the data needs to travel farther than the program.
Not all of it. Not always publicly. Not without permission. Not without care. But conceptually, the data must be able to
This is where linked data belongs in the story of the Web.
The URL made documents reachable.
HTML made documents understandable enough for browsers to render and programs to inspect.
JavaScript made documents programmable.
Linked data asks whether the things described by those documents can become reachable and understandable too.
If they can, software gets a different shape. Applications do not have to be kingdoms that contain all meaning. They can
That is the bridge to the next chapter.

where should a person's own data live?
Wizard's fourteenth rule
The Web as home: your data should outlive your apps
Chapter 7 left a question hanging.
If the web becomes the operating system, who owns the home directory?
The previous two chapters added pressure from two sides. If innovation happens elsewhere, users should be able to benefi
On a normal computer the home-directory question has an obvious answer, at least in theory. There is a place where your
You can install a different text editor without rewriting all your text files. You can open an image in another program.
The web grew up differently.
On the web, each application tends to bring its own little home with it. Your mail lives in the mail app. Your photos li
This is understandable. It is also strange.
The web's first great trick was that a document could point outside itself. A URL could cross from one machine to anothe
But while the software moved into the network, the user's home did not really come with it.
Instead we got thousands of private little castles.
The app owns the room
When an app owns both the interface and the data, the boundary is convenient for the app. The app knows where everything
This is why it keeps happening. The private castle is not only greed. It is also easy architecture.
But it creates a familiar kind of trap. If the app disappears, your data may disappear with it. If the app changes direc
The problem is not merely that companies are sometimes badly behaved. The deeper problem is that the boundary is in the
The app has become the container for the user's life.
A wizard should flinch at that.

This does not make the problem easy. It may even make some parts harder. Permissions become more important. Shared vocab
But that is also the point.
Small, replaceable software requires a place where the important things are not owned by the software.
A web with a home directory
There is a project called Solid that tries to explore this direction. I do not want to turn this chapter into a manual f
Solid asks what would happen if identity and data were separated from applications. A person has an identity on the web.
This sounds almost too obvious, once said aloud. Of course your contacts should be yours. Of course your calendar should
And yet the obvious thing is not how most of the web works.
Because the current shape was easier to build.
Because users follow convenience.
Because developers follow the path with the fewest missing standards.
Because every app that becomes successful has an incentive to become a world.
This is where the earlier chapters start to matter. A URL is powerful because it lets worlds point at each other. HTML i
The personal web needs the same kind of thinking.

You need boundaries that make replacement normal.
Small tools, not kingdoms
If data and identity can outlive applications, software can become smaller.
A notes app does not have to become a complete platform for every thought you will ever have. It can be a good editor fo
This is the Lego castle trying to escape from the knitted castle again.
Of course, it will not escape completely. Real software always grows threads. Applications need assumptions. Data shapes
But the direction matters.
A private app castle asks: how can we keep the user inside?
A personal web asks: how can this tool remain useful after the user replaces it?

Wizard's fifteenth rule
Rule zero: there are no rules
By now the book has left a trail of wizard's rules behind it. Steal good ideas. Choose representations that do work for
These are useful rules. They are also traps, if you start treating them like commandments.
A rule is only a compressed story about something that worked often enough to be worth remembering. The moment the rule
So keep the rules close enough to use them, and loose enough to break them.
